title: The Big Ideas in Getting Things Done
attribution: An original summary of the ideas in Getting Things Done by David Allen (2001). Not affiliated with or endorsed by the author or publisher. If these ideas resonate, buy the full book.
summary:
Somewhere in your head right now there is a dentist appointment you have not booked, a reply you owe a colleague, and a vague unease about the state of the garage. David Allen's Getting Things Done, published in 2001 and still the book productivity coaches hand out first, opens with a blunt diagnosis: the stress you feel is not caused by having too much to do. It is caused by storing your commitments in the one place spectacularly unsuited to holding them — your brain. This companion walks through the strongest ideas in Allen's method at the level of principles, so you can see why a system built two decades before smartphones still runs underneath half the productivity apps on yours.

# Your head is a terrible office

Allen's foundational claim is that the mind generates well and archives badly. Every commitment you have made and not yet resolved — he thinks of these as open loops — sits in your head as a low-grade background process, burning attention you could be spending on the work in front of you. Worse, the brain has no sense of timing. It will nag you about buying stamps at midnight and stay silent when you walk past the post office. It replays the unbooked dentist appointment during your daughter's recital and forgets it again the moment you could actually act.

The deeper point is psychological, and it is the book's sharpest insight: every loop you are tracking mentally is an agreement you have made with yourself, and every agreement you are not keeping quietly erodes your own self-trust. You feel guilty about the garage not because the garage matters much, but because some part of you knows you promised yourself you would deal with it. Allen argues you have exactly three honest exits from that guilt: drop the commitment deliberately, renegotiate it, or finish it. What you cannot do — what most of us try to do — is keep the promise vague and hope the discomfort fades. It never does.

# Catch everything in a trusted net

The first practical move, then, is ruthless and total capture. Allen wants every commitment, idea, worry, and half-formed intention out of your head and into a small number of external containers you actually empty — a notebook, an inbox tray, a phone app, it genuinely does not matter which. What matters is completeness and trust. Your brain will only release its grip on an open loop when it believes, at a level deeper than logic, that the loop is recorded somewhere it will reliably resurface.

This is why partial systems fail. If your list holds ninety percent of your commitments, your mind still has to track the other ten percent, which means it has to keep scanning all of it, which means you have gained almost nothing. Allen's standard is closer to a full mental inventory: the work proposal and the burned-out lightbulb in the hallway, captured side by side. People who do a complete sweep for the first time routinely surface dozens or hundreds of items, and most report the same paradoxical result — seeing the full pile written down feels lighter than carrying an unknown fraction of it invisibly.

# The two-minute rule and other fast moves

A capture habit only works if the containers get emptied, and emptying them means making decisions, not just shuffling paper. For each captured item, Allen has you ask whether it actually requires action. Most things do not: they are trash, reference material to file, or maybes to park on a someday list you revisit later. For the items that do require action, he offers the move that escaped the book and became public shorthand — if the action would take less than about two minutes, just do it immediately, because filing and tracking it would cost more than finishing it.

Everything longer gets deliberately routed: handed to someone better placed to do it and logged on a list of things you are waiting for, or deferred onto your own lists and calendar. The principle underneath the mechanics is that you touch each item once and decide, rather than re-reading the same email six times across a week. Allen's term for this stage is processing, and his insistence that deciding and doing are separate activities is half the system. Most clutter, physical and digital, is simply deferred decision-making made visible.

# The magic of the next physical action

Here is the idea that earns the book its reputation. Most to-do lists fail because their items are not actions at all — they are projects wearing disguises. You cannot do plan Mom's birthday or sort out the insurance. Faced with an undoable item, your brain does the rational thing: it flinches and moves on, and the item rolls forward week after week, gathering dread. Allen's fix is to force every item through one question: what is the very next physical, visible behavior that would move this forward? Not the goal, not the eventual outcome — the next concrete act. Call the restaurant. Find the policy number. Email Raj for the file.

The transformation is almost embarrassing in its effectiveness. Procrastination, in Allen's analysis, is rarely laziness; it is usually an undefined next step. Define the step at the level of physical behavior and the resistance largely evaporates, because the decision-making was the hard part and you have already done it. Allen pairs this with a planning habit at the other end of the telescope: for anything multi-step, get equally clear on what done looks like. An outcome you can picture plus a next action you can take is, he argues, the minimum viable plan for nearly everything in your life.

# Lists that match where you stand

Once items are clarified, Allen organizes them by a criterion most systems ignore: where and how you could actually do them. Rather than one monolithic list, he keeps next actions sorted by context — things requiring a phone, things requiring the office, errands requiring the car. The logic is that when you have twenty minutes and only a phone, a list of calls is useful and a list of everything is noise. The 2001 details feel dated now that one device is every context, but the principle survives: review only the actions you could take right now.

Two other structures do heavy lifting. A projects list serves as an index of every outcome you are committed to that needs more than one action, so nothing multi-step can silently stall. And the calendar gets treated as protected ground: only appointments and genuinely day-specific items live there, never the hopeful to-do list that half of us scribble onto Tuesday and then guiltily migrate to Wednesday. Allen's reasoning is that a calendar you actually believe is the difference between a tool and a wish list. Daily to-dos written on dates you will not honor train you to distrust your own system.

# The weekly tune-up

Every external system decays, and Allen's answer is the habit serious practitioners call the keystone of the whole method: a standing weekly review. Once a week, you empty your capture points, walk your projects list, make sure each project still has a live next action, clean up the calendar, and scan the someday list for anything whose moment has come. It typically takes an hour or two, and it is the part everyone skips first — which, Allen argues, is precisely why most adopters relapse.

The review matters because trust is the system's fuel. The moment your lists go stale, your brain detects it and quietly resumes the tracking job, and you are back to three a.m. reminders about stamps. The review is also where altitude comes in: with the runway of next actions in order, you can lift your gaze to longer horizons — responsibilities, yearly goals, the larger question of what all this activity is for. Allen is sometimes caricatured as a bottom-up mechanic, but his ordering is deliberate. You cannot think clearly about your life's direction, he insists, while two hundred unprocessed commitments are shouting from the basement.

# Calm is a competitive edge

What Allen is finally selling is not efficiency but a particular quality of presence — the ability to be fully engaged with whatever is in front of you because everything else is parked somewhere trustworthy. He noticed in 2001 what is now undeniable: knowledge work has no edges. No boss defines your inputs, the work is never visibly finished, and the volume of incoming claims on your attention only grows. In that world, the scarce skill is not working harder; it is maintaining a clear inventory of your commitments so that you are choosing your next move rather than reacting to whatever screams loudest.

Read the full book if you recognize yourself in the diagnosis: the mental churn at night, the inbox you are afraid of, the projects that stall for want of a defined next step. It rewards doers over browsers — Allen's chapters on the initial full-scale collection and the detailed mechanics of lists, filing, and review are written to be implemented, not skimmed. Managers, freelancers, students, and anyone whose job is essentially an unbounded stream of inputs will get the most from it. The prose is workmanlike; the payoff is a quieter head, and twenty-five years of devoted readers suggest he is keeping his end of the agreement.
summary_short:
The stress of modern work does not come from having too much to do. It comes, David Allen argues, from storing your commitments in the one place least suited to holding them: your head. Every unresolved promise runs there as an open loop, burning background attention — nagging you about stamps at midnight, then falling silent when you actually pass the post office. Worse, each loop is an agreement you made with yourself, and every agreement you are not keeping quietly erodes your own self-trust.

The method moves in stages. First, capture everything into a few external containers your brain genuinely trusts, because a system holding ninety percent of your commitments releases almost none of the load; the mind keeps scanning for the missing tenth. Then process by deciding rather than shuffling: trash it, file it, or park it on a someday list, and if an action takes under two minutes, do it now. The fix that earned the book its reputation is blunt. Most to-do lists fail because their items are projects in disguise, undoable as written, so name the next physical, visible behavior instead. Call the restaurant. Find the policy number. Resistance largely evaporates, because the deciding was the hard part. A weekly review keeps the machine trusted, since the moment your lists go stale your brain quietly resumes the tracking job.

The honest cost is real. The initial capture sweep can eat a full day, and the system is hardest to sustain for creative work, scattered attention, or roles where the next action will not sit still long enough to be named. But Allen's deepest claim survives that: you cannot think clearly about the direction of your life while two hundred unprocessed commitments shout from the basement. Every task app is a rounding error on that one insight.

learnings.text: Working memory is a generator, not a warehouse — it produces ideas and loses lists: every unresolved commitment runs as a background process that drains attention and resurfaces at useless moments.
learnings.chapter: Your head is a terrible office

learnings.text: Every mentally tracked to-do is an agreement with yourself, and unkept self-agreements erode self-trust; the only honest exits are dropping, renegotiating, or doing.
learnings.chapter: Your head is a terrible office

learnings.text: Capture works only when it is complete — a system holding ninety percent of your commitments still forces your brain to scan everything for the missing ten.
learnings.chapter: Catch everything in a trusted net

learnings.text: Deciding and doing are separate activities: touch each incoming item once, decide its fate, and most clutter — which is just deferred decision-making — disappears.
learnings.chapter: The two-minute rule and other fast moves

learnings.text: If an action takes under about two minutes, doing it on the spot is cheaper than the overhead of filing and tracking it.
learnings.chapter: The two-minute rule and other fast moves

learnings.text: Procrastination is usually an undefined next step, not laziness; naming the next physical, visible action dissolves most resistance because the hard decision is already made.
learnings.chapter: The magic of the next physical action

learnings.text: A pictured outcome plus a concrete next action is the minimum viable plan for almost anything multi-step in your life.
learnings.chapter: The magic of the next physical action

learnings.text: Keep the calendar sacred: only true appointments and day-specific items belong there, because daily to-dos you migrate forward train you to distrust your own system.
learnings.chapter: Lists that match where you stand

learnings.text: The weekly review is the keystone habit — the moment your lists go stale, your brain detects it and silently takes back the tracking job.
In edgeless knowledge work, the scarce advantage is a clear inventory of commitments that lets you choose your next move instead of reacting to the loudest input.

cards.title: Your Brain Has No Clock
cards.body: The mind nags you about stamps at midnight and goes silent at the post office. It stores commitments without any sense of timing, which is why mental to-do lists produce anxiety instead of action. Move reminders into an external system that surfaces them when you can actually act.

cards.title: Open Loops Tax Everything
cards.body: Every unresolved commitment you hold in your head runs as a background process, skimming attention from whatever you are doing now. The cost is invisible but constant. You do not feel one loop; you feel two hundred of them as a vague, permanent pressure you have stopped noticing.

cards.title: Unkept Promises Erode Self-Trust
cards.body: Each mental to-do is an agreement with yourself, and every one you ignore quietly damages your self-respect — even the trivial ones. You have three honest options: drop the commitment deliberately, renegotiate it, or do it. Vague guilt is what happens when you choose none of them.

cards.title: Capture Everything or Gain Nothing
cards.body: A list holding most of your commitments barely helps, because your brain must still scan for the missing ones. The benefit arrives only at completeness, when your mind finally trusts the net and lets go. Sweep work and home, big and trivial, into the same external inventory.

cards.title: Clutter Is Deferred Decisions
cards.body: The overflowing inbox and the chaotic desk are not storage problems; they are stacks of decisions you have postponed. Touch each item once and decide: trash it, file it, do it now, hand it off, or schedule it. The mess dissolves when the deciding does.

cards.title: Under Two Minutes? Do It
cards.body: When an action would take less than about two minutes, tracking it costs more than finishing it. Reply, confirm, file, toss — immediately. This single rule clears a startling share of daily volume and keeps your lists reserved for work that genuinely needs scheduling and thought.

cards.title: Projects Wear Disguises
cards.body: You cannot do sort out the insurance — it is a project pretending to be a task, and your brain rightly flinches. Force every list item through one filter: what is the next physical, visible action? Find the policy number is doable. Disguised projects just roll forward gathering dread.

cards.title: Name the Next Physical Action
cards.body: Procrastination is usually a missing decision, not a missing work ethic. When you define the very next concrete behavior — call this number, draft three sentences — resistance collapses, because the thinking was the hard part and it is already done. Stalled items almost always lack this definition.

cards.title: Keep the Calendar Sacred
cards.body: Put only appointments and genuinely day-specific items on your calendar — never the optimistic to-do list you will migrate to tomorrow. A calendar you always honor becomes a tool you trust at a glance. One polluted with wishes trains you to ignore everything written on it.

cards.title: Review Weekly or Relapse
cards.body: Lists decay, and the moment yours go stale your brain resumes the tracking job you built the system to escape. A standing weekly review — empty capture points, refresh every project's next action, scan the horizon — is the maintenance that keeps the entire method alive.

cards.title: Clear the Runway First
cards.body: Big-picture reflection fails while two hundred unprocessed commitments shout from below. Get the ground level in order — actions defined, lists current — and the mental space for genuine questions about direction and purpose opens naturally. Control at the bottom is what makes vision at the top possible.

cards.title: Presence Is the Real Prize
cards.body: The method's endpoint is not a tidy list; it is full engagement with whatever is in front of you, because everything else is parked somewhere trustworthy. In work with no edges, calm attention — not raw effort — is the scarce resource worth building a system to protect.

actions.title: Set up your capture tools
actions.detail: Choose one physical inbox tray and one digital capture app, and put a notepad where ideas strike you. Allen's method starts with frictionless, always-available places to park open loops the moment they appear.

actions.title: Do a full mind sweep
actions.detail: Write down every commitment, worry, and half-formed intention you are carrying — work and personal, huge and trivial — until nothing else surfaces. Completeness is the point: your brain only relaxes when the inventory is total.

actions.title: Process your sweep to zero
actions.detail: Go item by item: trash it, file it as reference, do it now if it takes under two minutes, delegate it, or route it to a list. Decide once per item — re-reading without deciding is how piles form.

actions.title: Build your projects list
actions.detail: List every outcome you are committed to that needs more than one action, then give each a pictured end state. This index is what keeps multi-step work from silently stalling between sessions.

actions.title: Define a next action for every project
actions.detail: For each project, name the next physical, visible behavior — a call, an email, a search. Allen's core fix for procrastination is making the decision in advance so future-you only has to act.

actions.title: Purge your calendar of wishes
actions.detail: Strip the calendar down to real appointments and genuinely day-specific items, moving everything else to your action lists. A calendar you always honor is one you can trust at a glance.

actions.title: Run the two-minute rule on email
actions.detail: Process your inbox oldest-first, immediately doing anything under two minutes and routing the rest to lists or an archive. Practice the touch-it-once discipline until your inbox reaches empty.

actions.title: Hold your first weekly review
actions.detail: Empty all capture points, walk the projects list to confirm each has a live next action, clean the calendar, and scan your someday items. This is the keystone habit that keeps the whole system trustworthy.

actions.title: Create a waiting-for list
actions.detail: Log everything you have delegated or are expecting from others, with dates. Tracking other people's open loops is how you follow up calmly instead of remembering in a panic.

actions.title: Run review two and lock the ritual
actions.detail: Repeat the full weekly review, note where the system decayed during the week, and book the review as a permanent calendar slot. Two consecutive reviews is the difference between an experiment and a practice.
